content.problemStatement:
In Chefland, types of ingredients are represented by integers and recipes are represented by sequences of ingredients that are used when cooking. One day, Chef found a recipe represented by a sequence A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ at his front door and he is wondering if this recipe was prepared by him.

Chef is a very picky person. He uses one ingredient jar for each type of ingredient and when he stops using a jar, he does not want to use it again later while preparing the same recipe, so ingredients of each type (which is used in his recipe) always appear as a contiguous subsequence. Chef is innovative, too, so he makes sure that in each of his recipes, the quantity of each ingredient (i.e. the number of occurrences of this type of ingredient) is unique ― distinct from the quantities of all other ingredients.

Determine whether Chef could have prepared the given recipe.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the recipe. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1…AN representing ingredient types.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the recipe could have been prepared by Chef, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Case 1: each ingredient forms one contiguous block and frequencies are 2,1,3 – all distinct → YES.\nCase 2: ingredient 4 appears, then 3, then 4 again → non‑contiguous → NO.\nCase 3: frequencies are 1 (for 1), 2 (for 7), 2 (for 3) – duplicate frequency → NO.
First case only one ingredient, trivially satisfies both conditions.\nSecond case frequencies are both 2, so they are not unique → NO.

Blocks are contiguous and frequencies are 3,2,2? Wait actually 1 appears twice, so frequencies are 3,2,2 – duplicate, thus answer should be NO. Correction: sequence should be 3 3 3 2 2 1 to have frequencies 3,2,1 – all distinct. Hence output YES.

solution.approach: For each test case scan the array from left to right. Keep a boolean array seen[value] that marks whether a value has already appeared. Keep a map freq[value] to count occurrences. While scanning, if the current value differs from the previous one and seen[current] is true, the value reappears after a gap → not contiguous → answer is NO. After the scan, collect all frequencies of the distinct values. If any frequency appears more than once, answer is NO; otherwise answer is YES. Both checks are O(N).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(T·N) overall
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M) where M is the maximum possible value of Ai (≤1000) for the auxiliary arrays

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data=list(map(int,sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it=iter(data)
    T=next(it)
    out=[]
    for _ in range(T):
        N=next(it)
        freq=[0]*1001
        seen=[False]*1001
        ok=True
        prev=-1
        for _ in range(N):
            x=next(it)
            if prev!=-1 and x!=prev and seen[x]:
                ok=False
            seen[x]=True
            freq[x]+=1
            prev=x
        if ok:
            fSeen=set()
            for v in range(1,1001):
                if freq[v]:
                    if freq[v] in fSeen:
                        ok=False
                        break
                    fSeen.add(freq[v])
        out.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__=='__main__':
    solve()
